The Map Gallery

Every map the toolkit has built — 57 in all — each one an experiment in honest, directable historical cartography. Six visual styles are distributed deliberately across the fleet (the badge on each card says which, and each map's page explains why). Click a card for the living map; open the accordions under each family for the ideas.

48 corpus data maps · 3 curated journeys · 6 curated networks
Modern AtlasCopperplate EngravingIlluminated ManuscriptAlchemical NoirWoodcut & RubricLapis & Gold
The Guided Tour9 scrollytelling chapters — start here Instruction Manualdirecting, reading & sharing the maps Developers Toolkitcontracts, engines, APIs, extending Next Stepswhere the project goes from here
Directed ExamplesThe Scholarship ShelfSix ErasEleven CenturiesSeven ThemesEight FiguresSix RegionsJourneysNetworks

Directed Examples

One-sentence directives carried end-to-end: sentence → MapSpec → map. The Paracelsian map is the project's founding example; the others test the authoring loop on fresh questions — including a map whose deliberate sparseness is its argument.

The MapSpec compiler
The core idea of the whole project: a small JSON recipe sits between fuzzy natural language and deterministic rendering. You (or a Claude Code skill) describe a map; the description becomes a versionable contract; a Python generator compiles it to a finished artifact. Change one word in the recipe and rebuild — the variation is reproducible, diffable, and honest about what was asked for. Every data map in this gallery has its recipe linked at the top of its page.

Read more: Toolkit §3 — the MapSpec contract · Manual §3 — directing a map in plain language

Period-accurate borders
Early-modern events used to float over 21st-century borders. Now a spec can say historical_boundaries_year: 1560 and the compiler embeds approximate period polities from a locally cached, slimmed historical-basemaps dataset (snapped to the nearest cached year). It ships as a toggleable Borders layer — hover a line for the polity's name — and the map automatically gains a caveat: approximate, for orientation, never authority.

Read more: Toolkit §4 — fetch_boundaries.py · Manual §8 — borders are orientation, not authority

Provenance or it doesn't compile
Every engine refuses unsourced content: a MapSpec without a provenance note, a journey stop without evidence + source, a network edge without type/evidence/citation — all are build errors, not warnings. The historian's hard line, made mechanical. The note you see in each map's panel is the artifact of that rule.

Read more: Toolkit §1 — architecture & principles · Manual §10 — 'provenance_note is required'

Layered controls — lenses, filters, drawer
Controls like a search engine: newcomers get five one-click lenses (Transmission · Lives · Texts · Themes · Density) that reconfigure everything at once; regulars get a filter bar (theme, colour-by, layer chips); power users get an advanced drawer (widths, sizes, opacity, tooltips). The bet: progressive disclosure beats both the empty-map-with-99-toggles and the locked-down infographic.

Read more: Manual §4 — reading a data map

The theme engine — four faces
One map, four complete visual identities, switchable live: Modern Atlas (clarity), Copperplate Engraving (the printed corpus), Illuminated Manuscript (courts and gold), Alchemical Noir (single lives, spectacle). Colour is assigned at runtime from the active theme's palette, so re-skinning never touches the data. The experiment: does aesthetic register change what a reader trusts and notices? Try the same map in Atlas and Noir.

Read more: Toolkit §7 — the theme engine

34 events · 8 places · 10 arcs · borders c. 1530
corpus data map Illuminated Manuscript

Women in the Art

The female practitioners the record kept — and how few it kept

8 events · 2 places · 0 arcs · borders c. 500

The Scholarship Shelf

One map per most-cited study in the collection's own historiography — the study's key topics and figures scoped from the corpus, styled to its source base, citation counts in the provenance. Principe, Newman & Principe, Smith, Hanegraaff, Fowden, Copenhaver, Pereira.

Maps keyed to the scholarship
The collection knows its own historiography: 43 modern scholars appear in it, and their surname citations across the 680 events are countable (Principe 90 · Newman 60 · Smith 21 · Hanegraaff 19 · Fowden 15 · Copenhaver 13 · Pereira 8). The Scholarship Shelf gives each most-cited study its own map — the study's key topics and figures, scoped from the corpus, styled to the study's source base, with the citation count in the provenance line. A map as a book review.

Read more: Toolkit §4 — build_scholarship.py · Manual §8 — corpus vs. curated

Style as argument
Six full visual identities — Modern Atlas, Copperplate Engraving, Illuminated Manuscript, Alchemical Noir, Woodcut & Rubric, Lapis & Gold — and every map now opens in the one that suits its material: copperplate for print-culture subjects, lapis for late antiquity, noir for hidden and rejected knowledge, woodcut for scholastic polemic. Each map's page carries a design note saying why. The reader can always switch styles live — the assignment is a curatorial argument, not a lock.

Read more: Toolkit §7 — the theme engine · Manual §4 — the six styles

The data is the weather
The corpus under-tags conceptual themes (distillation: 237 events; iatrochemistry: 9). Rather than hide that, the toolkit surfaces it: the vocabulary tool prints per-slug event counts, and the compiler warns when a requested theme matches zero events. Sparse maps are shown as sparse, with the sparseness explained — the alternative is a lens that silently shows nothing.

Read more: Toolkit §4 — list_vocab.py · Manual §10 — sparse maps

Provenance or it doesn't compile
Every engine refuses unsourced content: a MapSpec without a provenance note, a journey stop without evidence + source, a network edge without type/evidence/citation — all are build errors, not warnings. The historian's hard line, made mechanical. The note you see in each map's panel is the artifact of that rule.

Read more: Toolkit §1 — architecture & principles · Manual §10 — 'provenance_note is required'

Six Eras

One map per major era of alchemy, from Greco-Egyptian Alexandria to English chymistry. Batch-authored by build_all.py; the tour's spine.

Style as argument
Six full visual identities — Modern Atlas, Copperplate Engraving, Illuminated Manuscript, Alchemical Noir, Woodcut & Rubric, Lapis & Gold — and every map now opens in the one that suits its material: copperplate for print-culture subjects, lapis for late antiquity, noir for hidden and rejected knowledge, woodcut for scholastic polemic. Each map's page carries a design note saying why. The reader can always switch styles live — the assignment is a curatorial argument, not a lock.

Read more: Toolkit §7 — the theme engine · Manual §4 — the six styles

The MapSpec compiler
The core idea of the whole project: a small JSON recipe sits between fuzzy natural language and deterministic rendering. You (or a Claude Code skill) describe a map; the description becomes a versionable contract; a Python generator compiles it to a finished artifact. Change one word in the recipe and rebuild — the variation is reproducible, diffable, and honest about what was asked for. Every data map in this gallery has its recipe linked at the top of its page.

Read more: Toolkit §3 — the MapSpec contract · Manual §3 — directing a map in plain language

Time as accumulation
The scrubber doesn't slice time, it accumulates it — press Play and events pile up through the years, so centres of gravity visibly form and migrate. On journeys the same control becomes progress-through-a-life. Under reduced-motion settings Play turns into a discrete step-through: same story, no animation.

Read more: Manual §4 — reading a data map

Transmission arcs — knowledge in motion
Arcs connect consecutive dated events of the same named person: alchemy moving as people moved. The honesty cost is stated on every map: arcs trace named figures whose itineraries survive; the anonymous carriers — merchants, manuscripts, students — vastly outnumbered them, and absence of an arc is absence of record.

Read more: Manual §8 — absence ≠ absence

Period-accurate borders
Early-modern events used to float over 21st-century borders. Now a spec can say historical_boundaries_year: 1560 and the compiler embeds approximate period polities from a locally cached, slimmed historical-basemaps dataset (snapped to the nearest cached year). It ships as a toggleable Borders layer — hover a line for the polity's name — and the map automatically gains a caveat: approximate, for orientation, never authority.

Read more: Toolkit §4 — fetch_boundaries.py · Manual §8 — borders are orientation, not authority

Eleven Centuries

The 8th through 18th centuries as transmission maps. Scroll them in order and the centre of gravity migrates: Baghdad → al-Andalus → Latin Europe → the north.

Seven Themes

One conceptual or operational theme each, traced across the whole corpus — chosen from tags that are actually populated (the counts are part of the story).

Eight Figures

One practitioner's evented footprint each, with itinerary arcs. These read the corpus; for sourced life-routes see the Journeys.

Style as argument
Six full visual identities — Modern Atlas, Copperplate Engraving, Illuminated Manuscript, Alchemical Noir, Woodcut & Rubric, Lapis & Gold — and every map now opens in the one that suits its material: copperplate for print-culture subjects, lapis for late antiquity, noir for hidden and rejected knowledge, woodcut for scholastic polemic. Each map's page carries a design note saying why. The reader can always switch styles live — the assignment is a curatorial argument, not a lock.

Read more: Toolkit §7 — the theme engine · Manual §4 — the six styles

Transmission arcs — knowledge in motion
Arcs connect consecutive dated events of the same named person: alchemy moving as people moved. The honesty cost is stated on every map: arcs trace named figures whose itineraries survive; the anonymous carriers — merchants, manuscripts, students — vastly outnumbered them, and absence of an arc is absence of record.

Read more: Manual §8 — absence ≠ absence

Curated layers beside the corpus
The database has no reliable relational or itinerary data, so journeys and networks are hand-curated from real scholarship, every item sourced, sitting beside the corpus rather than pretending to be derived from it. Data maps visualize the database; curated maps visualize the literature. Each page says which kind you're reading.

Read more: Toolkit §5 — curated data formats · Manual §8 — corpus vs. curated

Maps that travel honestly
🔗 Link copies a URL reproducing the exact view (theme, lens, layers, year, sliders); ⤓ PNG exports the current view with title, year, and the borders caveat burnt into a footer strip. The idea: when a map leaves the toolkit — into a slide, a tweet, a paper — its honesty apparatus should refuse to be left behind.

Read more: Manual §7 — sharing and exporting

Journeys

Lives as routes: hand-curated, per-stop sourced, self-drawing, ending withheld. The uncertainty-treatment selector is the experiment here.

The withheld ending
The journey draws itself stop by stop and never renders future stops — you can pause mid-life and genuinely not know what comes. An experiment in narrative cartography: maps usually spoil their own endings by showing the whole route at once; biography read that way loses its contingency. Here the road ahead is dark, as it was.

Read more: Manual §5 — reading a journey

Four uncertainty treatments
The same journey ships four editorial stances, switchable live: Lay (clean story), Understated (quiet solid/dashed apparatus), Scholar (citations and gap notes), Centerpiece (doubt made visible — ghost routes, a running 'what we don't know' panel). The experiment: uncertainty display is an editorial choice, so make it a control instead of a dogma.

Read more: Manual §5 — reading a journey

Evidence in the line itself
A consistent visual grammar everywhere: line style = evidence (solid documented, dashed reconstructed), opacity = confidence, colour = category — and never colour for confidence, the commonest map lie. Every stop, arc, and edge carries its source on hover.

Read more: Manual §8 — how to read honestly

Curated layers beside the corpus
The database has no reliable relational or itinerary data, so journeys and networks are hand-curated from real scholarship, every item sourced, sitting beside the corpus rather than pretending to be derived from it. Data maps visualize the database; curated maps visualize the literature. Each page says which kind you're reading.

Networks

Constellations of people: typed, weighted, evidence-bearing ties. Six subjects in one explorer; the survival-bias toggle is the point.

The survival-bias toggle
Correspondence networks are survival maps pretending to be sociability maps: whoever's archive survived looks central. The toggle makes the lie visible — 'show only what survives' drops reconstructed ties and watches the network collapse toward the archive-keepers. The distortion is the exhibit.

Read more: Manual §6 — reading a network

The centre is a choice
Click any figure to pivot the network around them: their ties light, the rest dims. There is no privileged centre — Rudolf II's Prague looks different centred on Kelley than on the emperor. A small interaction against the oldest network-diagram fallacy.

Read more: Manual §6 — reading a network

Evidence in the line itself
A consistent visual grammar everywhere: line style = evidence (solid documented, dashed reconstructed), opacity = confidence, colour = category — and never colour for confidence, the commonest map lie. Every stop, arc, and edge carries its source on hover.

MAPRESEARCH — an R&D project in honest, directable historical cartography. Borders are approximate; absence is absence of record; the maps say so.
